item.title: Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke; Leo Platform npm Packages Compromised
item.content:encoded:
Mass npm Supply Chain Attack: 20 Leo Platform Packages Compromised — Must Read

Twenty Leo Platform npm packages were compromised in a coordinated supply-chain attack that used a stealthy binding.gyp install hook to steal CI/CD, cloud, registry, and password-manager secrets from GitHub Actions environments. The campaign appears tied to the earlier Miasma operation and can also propagate by abusing stolen npm tokens to publish more malicious packages.

  • On June 24, 2026, an attacker published malicious versions of 20 npm packages in the Leo Platform ecosystem within a window of less than three seconds.
  • The compromised packages contain a CI/CD attack toolkit that steals secrets from GitHub Actions runners, cloud credential stores, package registries, and password managers, then exfiltrates them using the victim’s own GitHub token.
  • The Leo Platform attack uses the same toolkit and delivery mechanism documented in the earlier Miasma campaign, including a Phantom Gyp binding.gyp install hook, a three-layer obfuscation chain, and Bun runtime evasion.
  • The payload can locate the GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process, read process memory to recover masked secrets, sweep credentials from AWS, GCP, Azure, HashiCorp Vault, Kubernetes, npm, PyPI, RubyGems, JFrog, GitHub PATs, and 1Password, and modify GitHub Actions workflow files.
  • The affected malicious versions include leo-logger@1.0.8, leo-sdk@6.0.19, leo-aws@2.0.4, leo-config@1.1.1, leo-streams@2.0.1, serverless-leo@3.0.14, and 14 other Leo Platform packages listed in the report.

New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns — Must Read

A new stealthy in-memory backdoor called Mistic has been linked to the KongTuke initial access broker and is being delivered through ClickFix-style campaigns alongside ModeloRAT. The activity appears aimed at gaining low-visibility access to organizations that can be sold to ransomware affiliates, including those tied to Qilin.

  • A new stealthy backdoor named Mistic, also tracked as MLTBackdoor, has been deployed in suspected financially motivated attacks against organizations in the insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors since April 2026.
  • Symantec and Carbon Black linked Mistic to the KongTuke initial access broker and reported that it has been dropped alongside ModeloRAT, a Python remote access trojan previously attributed to the group.
  • Mistic uses DLL side-loading with the trusted Microsoft endpoint security tool MpExtMs.exe, runs payloads directly in memory, and includes a kill switch that can terminate and delete itself.
  • The backdoor can upload, download, move, rename, or delete files, create folders, change its command polling interval, execute code from its command-and-control server in memory, and load Beacon Object Files.
  • Broadcom said the targeting appears opportunistic and noted that ModeloRAT has been observed in attacks that deployed Qilin ransomware.
